items.title: About Me
items.text:
I’ve spent over a decade in the IT world, but my foundation is actually in the "old school" of content: I have a Master’s in book publishing and text editing. That background gave me a deep respect for information architecture, copyright, and the life cycle of a project—whether it’s a printed textbook or a digital knowledge base.

I’ve worked across support, instruction, and knowledge management, collaborating with everyone from DEV and QA to Sales, Marketing and HR. I don't just write; I find the right tone, style, and complexity for each audience.

items.title: What I do
items.text:
I’m a bit of a "bridge between SMEs and users." I know your developers and product owners are busy. My job is to join team meetings, schedule short 1-1s, listen actively, and turn "tribal knowledge" into structured, comprehensive documentation. I don't just "write manuals"—I help your busiest team members reclaim their time.

Beyond standard internal and customer-facing docs, I can help your team refine technical requirements, create release notes, and manage the complexities of white-labeling, rebranding, and global expansion.

items.title: Bringing order to a growing product
items.text: Currently, I am a Technical Writer in a Product department. I joined soon after the company moved past the "startup" stage. My main challenge is that almost no product documentation existed when I arrived. I act as a "knowledge detective"—I talk to the team, find the missing information, building the glossary and relentlessly push everyone to use the same terms. As I document the system, I also look for ways to automate the maintanance process and semi-automate the creation of guides and tutorials to save time for everyone.

items.title: Managing Complexity and AI
items.text:
In my previous role as a Knowledge Manager, I looked after a customer-facing knowledge base with over 1,000 articles. This was a massive project that involved moving content between different platforms and merging information as our company grew and bought other businesses.

I also worked directly on AI implementation, specifically setting up RAG search and automated translation. I’ve learned how to structure information so it’s easy for a human to read, but also "machine-readable" for an AI or LLM.

items.title: Navigating Languages and Cultures
items.text: Through my years in Technical Support—moving from agent to instructor and eventually instructional designer—I’ve mastered the art of leading a conversation, listen actively, set expectations and ask the right questions. I sucessfully collaborated with people from North and Central America, CIS countries, Georgia, India, and Japan. I’ve learned that the key to a successful project is often how you navigate the interpersonal nuances. I speak Russian and English fluently, but more importantly, I speak the "languages" of Support, QA, Marketing, and Engineering and respect the cultural background of every colleague and partner.

items.title: Learning New Industries Quickly
items.text: I understand the new hire onboarding process inside and out: I’ve personally managed the training and onboarding for over 50 new hires. In my most recent role, I mastered a completely new industry and a new set of tools in just a few weeks. I don't wait to be "fully trained" to help—I was already delivering useful documents during my first month.

items.title: Documentation & Content Strategy
items.text:
• Methodology: SME-Led Documentation, Product-Centric Documentation, Docs-as-Code, Information Architecture (Diataxis), Searchability Analysis, manual testing.
  • Static Site Generators: MkDocs, Zensical, Docsify, Git-based workflows.
  • Authoring & Markup: Markdown, HTML, Obsidian, VS Code, Microsoft Word.
  • Platforms: Confluence, Oracle CX Knowledge, Google Workspace.
  • Technical Illustration: Creating clear block schemes and flowcharts to explain complex system architectures (e.g., Mail Server structures). Tools: Mermaid, draw.io, whiteboard tools.

items.title: Learning & Instructional Design
items.text:
• Frameworks: ADDIE, Bloom’s Taxonomy, Exam Design for global audiences.
  • LMS Administration: Docebo, Moodle.
  • Learning Content Authoring: Articulate 360 (Storyline, Rise), Navattic.
  • Media Production: Animation and Video (Vyond, Snagit, Camtasia), Audio (writing scripts, recordign and editing narration).

items.title: Technical Knowledge
items.text:
• Infrastructure: Networking (TCP/IP, DNS), Mail Servers (Exchange, POP/IMAP), Spam Filters, Encryption.
  • Cloud & Connectivity: IaaS (Virtual Servers, Web Hosting), SaaS (Web, Desktop, and Mobile), VoIP Telephony, Unified Communications.
  • Security & Access: 2FA, Single Sign-On (SSO), Role Based Access to Control Panels, IP Whitelist/Block List management.
  • APIs: REST API, AsyncAPI, GraphQL
  • AI: RAG Search implementation, preparing content for Automated Translation, building custom GPTs/Gems, building AI agents and related automations.

items.title: Collaboration & Management
items.text:
• Frameworks: Agile, Scrum, Waterfall, Feedback Management.
  • Tools: Jira, Smartsheet, Slack, Microsoft Teams.
  • Reporting: Building tables and charts in Excel, building dashboards (Jira, Oracle CX Cloud)

items.title: Industry Experience
items.text:
• Telecommunications: Support and documentation for complex infrastructure and hardware.
  • EdTech: Administrating Learning Management Systems (LMS), creating learning paths, instructional videos, eLearning courses and knowledge assessments, proctoring exams.
  • Sports Betting: Documenting fast-changing products and their features, back-office tools and reports, and user flows.
  • Publishing: Full book creation and distribution cycle (both print and digital).

items.title: Publishing Studies and Content Strategy
items.text:
Master of Arts | Bachelor of Arts | 2011 — 2018 Saint Petersburg State University of Industrial Technologies and Design

I treat documentation as a complex product. My 7-year academic track provided a 360-degree view of the content lifecycle—from the structural integrity of typography and HTML to the legal and economic frameworks of distribution.

Research Focus: Digital Transformation of Educational Content

My thesis and practicum focused on converting traditional print textbooks into interactive digital ecosystems. I developed frameworks for integrating legacy media (audio/disc) with modern interactive elements, a methodology I now apply to scaling corporate knowledge bases and technical documentation.

items.title: Methods and Techniques of Teaching Online
items.text:
Continuous Professional Education Diploma | 2021 Moscow Institute of Psychoanalysis

Focus: Instructional design, psychological aspects of remote knowledge transfer, presentation methods for online learning, Bloom's Taxonomy, ADDIE model, curriculum development, building knowledge asessments.

items.title: OpenStreetMap
items.text: Adding houses and points of interest to OpenStreetMap across Georgia, documented the Georgian community naming conventions and data sources.

items.title: Wikipedia
items.text: Translating articles about Tbilisi neighbourhoods.

items.title: API Documentation
items.text: While my primary focus is on product and internal documentation, I am comfortable working within technical environments to verify data. I use API tools to ensure my documentation accurately reflects how the system behaves "under the hood."

items.title: REST API, OpenAPI, Swagger
items.text:
On a regular basis, I use Swagger UI to explore the endpoints of the projects I document. I am familiar with:

  • Testing: Authenticating and sending requests to verify real-world responses.
  • Schema Improvement: As part of my professional development, I have experience enhancing OpenAPI schemas by adding descriptive titles, summaries, and clear examples to make them more developer-friendly.

items.title: GraphQL
items.text:
I have a general understanding of GraphQL and how it differs from the REST API.

As part of my regular duties on the project, I am required to mention the GraphQL endpoints and parameters, which I do by reviewing the GraphiQL auto-generated document that lists all parameters. So far, improving this representation is outside of my responsibilities, so I only look up specific names to mention them in technical requirements or in product documents.

items.title: AsyncAPI
items.text: I am familiar with the AsyncAPI specification for event-driven architectures. I recognize the message-driven systems documentation and can navigate these schemas to extract the information needed for high-level technical overviews. I have also built the glossary accompanying asyncAPI and enriched the schema with descriptions and examples.

items.title: Document Types
items.text:
My work included a wide range of materials:

  • Hardware quick start guides: Using vendor’s guides for desk phones and PDF documents created by the marketing operations team, wrote the articles with enhanced formatting adapted for machine translation and AI adoption.
  • How-to Guides: Developed comprehensive how-to guides covering installation, application usage, and system management, empowering users to effectively utilize our products.
  • Product Landing Pages: Developed and maintained engaging landing pages for the Support Center, ensuring easy navigation and access to critical information.
  • Release Notes: Proofread, enhanced, and published notes provided by Project managers.
  • Customer-Focused Video Tutorials: Produced engaging video tutorials that simplify complex technical concepts and provide step-by-step instructions for common tasks.

items.title: Goal-Oriented How-To Guides
items.text:
These articles are designed to help users achieve a specific task as quickly as possible. This includes installation, application usage, and system management.

Business Goal

To reduce the volume of basic support tickets by empowering users and admins to handle setup and management independently.

Obstacles and Challenges

My Role

Lead Technical Writer and Content Creator.

What I did to achieve it

I developed a comprehensive library of step-by-step guides. I focused on clear, action-oriented headings and used a consistent structure so that users could skim the document and find the specific "how-to" step they needed without reading the entire page.

items.title: Product Landing Pages (Explanation)
items.text:
Before a user can "do," they need to "understand." These pages serve as conceptual hubs for our products.

Business Goal

To provide a high-level overview of product ecosystems, ensuring users understand how different features work together before they dive into technical tasks.

Obstacles and Challenges

Information was often fragmented across different departments. I had to consolidate technical details from Product Owners and Marketing into a single, cohesive "source of truth."

My Role

Knowledge Manager / Information Architect.

What I did to achieve it

I designed and maintained landing pages that acted as a "map" for the Support Center. By organizing content conceptually, I improved site navigation and ensured that critical information was only one or two clicks away for any given product.

items.title: Technical Specifications & Hardware (Reference)
items.text:
These are "fact-based" documents, such as hardware quick start guides for desk phones and software release notes.

Business Goal

To provide reliable, searchable technical facts that are easy for both humans and AI-driven support tools to process.

Obstacles and Challenges

Original vendor guides were usually static PDFs that were difficult to search and didn't play well with machine translation (MT) or AI tools.

My Role

Technical Writer and Editor.

What I did to achieve it

I reformatted hardware guides into web-native articles. I used "clean" formatting specifically optimized for machine translation and AI adoption. For release notes, I translated raw technical updates from Project Managers into user-friendly summaries that highlighted key fixes and new features.

items.title: Visual Learning (Tutorials)
items.text:
For complex configurations, sometimes a video is more effective than a long text document.

Business Goal

To simplify high-level technical concepts (like mail protection policies) for visual learners, reducing the perceived complexity of the product.

Obstacles and Challenges

Identifying which topics actually needed video support required deep-diving into customer feedback and support trends.

My Role

Video Producer and Scriptwriter.

What I did to achieve it

I produced a series of tutorials by analyzing feedback to identify "pain points." I handled the entire pipeline: storyboarding, writing scripts, coordinating voiceovers with native speakers, and final video editing (including closed captions) to ensure the content was accessible to everyone.

items.title: Knowledge Base Management
items.text: During my ten years at Technical Support department at Intermedia Communications, I was contributing to their knowledge base in many ways. At first I was only writing articles which followed the style guide to address feedback or questions received from customers via Support channels. Later I took part in Knowledge Management projects as assistant to a knowledge manager, later fully took over knowledge manager responsibilities.

items.title: The Knowledge Base Goals and Specifics
items.text:
The knowledge base I worked with covered about 20 products and over 1,300 articles at max. Some products came from the aquired companies and therefor articles covering such products were not aligned with knowledge base syle guide and formatting which caused troubles.

White Label Strategy

The knowledge base served a white-label strategy covering five different brands, with different set of products available for each. The KMS hosted the content with variables and published it on five different sites replacing them with branded product names and using any parts that were unique for each brand.

Acievement

I had to miticuosly profread the article drafts to ensure that each brand-specific configuration is provided with the

Examples of how I approached different types of documentation to ensure users find exactly what they need are provided on the next page

items.title: The Knowledge Management System (KMS) tool
items.text:
The Knowledge Base at first was using a simple self-hosted core for hosting and publishing articles, supporting variables for white-label strategy.

Challenge

The authoring tool had a simple and very limited WYSWYG editor.

  • While articles native to this knowledge base were displaying fine, the articles migrated from other systems as part of company acquisition often had various issues with searchability or display.
  • Some articles needed to use a custom formatting, which is supported by the system, but not offered in the authoring tool.

Achievement

I worked with the source HTML code of articles and corrected the HTML tags to align with the formatting acceptable for the KMS.

items.title: Headers
items.text:
# H1 Header
## H2 Header
### H3 Header
#### H4 Header
##### H5 Header
###### H6 Header

items.title: Text formatting
items.text:
**bold text**
*italic text*
***bold and italic***
~~strikethrough~~
`inline code`

items.title: Links and images
items.text:
[Link text](https://example.com)
[Link with title](https://example.com "Hover title")
![Alt text](image.jpg)
![Image with title](image.jpg "Image title")

items.title: Lists
items.text:
Unordered:
- Item 1
- Item 2
  - Nested item

Ordered:
1. First item
2. Second item
3. Third item

items.title: Blockquotes
items.text:
> This is a blockquote
> Multiple lines
>> Nested quote

items.title: Code blocks
items.text:
```javascript
function hello() {
  console.log("Hello, world!");
}
```

items.title: Tables
items.text:
| Header 1 | Header 2 | Header 3 |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Row 1    | Data     | Data     |
| Row 2    | Data     | Data     |

items.title: Horizontal rule
items.text:
---
or
***
or
___

items.title: Task lists
items.text:
- [x] Completed task
- [ ] Incomplete task
- [ ] Another task

items.title: Escaping characters
items.text: Use backslash to escape: \* \_ \# \`

items.title: Line breaks
items.text:
End a line with two spaces  
to create a line break.

Or use a blank line for a new paragraph.
